preamble: The shift from a voiceless uvular plosive [q] to a voiced uvular fricative [ʁ] is a clear instance of lenition involving both voicing and spirantization. This change is common in environments where the airflow is less restricted, such as between vowels.

examples.note: In many Sudanese dialects, the classical /q/ is realized as a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.
Uvular plosives regularly lenite to fricatives when occurring between vowels.
Systematic lenition of uvular stops in suffixal positions or back-vowel contexts.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
